facts.label: WON WITHOUT PLAYING
facts.story: In 1958 qualifying, the Arab League boycott meant Egypt, Sudan, Turkey, Indonesia, and Syria all refused to face Israel, handing them the Asia-Africa group by default without kicking a ball. FIFA balked at sending a team to the World Cup that hadn't played a single match, so it ordered a playoff against Wales. Wales won both legs 2–0, in Ramat Gan and Cardiff, and took Israel's spot in Sweden instead.

facts.label: SPIEGLER'S ONE MOMENT
facts.story: Twelve years later Israel finally reached the finals, and on June 7, 1970 in Mexico City, captain Mordechai Spiegler met Sweden's 53rd-minute opener with an equalizer three minutes later — a 25-yard strike off his left foot. It remains, more than five decades on, the only goal Israel has ever scored at a World Cup.

1970.headline: Blue and White, Once

annotations.label: THE ONLY GOAL
annotations.story: Down 1–0 to Sweden in Mexico City, Spiegler struck from 25 yards in the 56th minute to level it 1–1. It's still the only goal Israel has scored across its lone World Cup appearance, more than 50 years later.

annotations.label: THE SHADE THAT CAME BACK
annotations.story: Israel's 1970 shirt, made by Umbro, was a lighter, almost powder blue rather than the richer royal blue the team wears today. When Puma took over the kit contract in 2018, designers dug up that pale 1970 shade and revived it — the only visual echo of Israel's lone World Cup on the current jersey.

annotations.label: BOYCOTTED AGAIN
annotations.story: Twelve years after a 1958 boycott cost them a World Cup berth, Israel hit the same wall again: North Korea refused to face them in 1970 qualifying and was expelled from the tournament for it. At the finals, FIFA quietly arranged the draw so Israel and Morocco, who'd threatened to walk out, never met.

1970.homeColors: blue shirt, white shorts, blue socks — Israel's traditional national colors, drawn from the flag
